segments.inferno-32-g18: Per ch’ is a contracted form (per + che) functioning here as a connective: 'whereupon' or 'so'. mi volsi is the passato remoto (volsi) with the reflexive clitic mi: 'I turned (myself)' — the clitic attaches to the finite verb as is typical in older/poetic Italian.
segments.inferno-32-g19: vidimi is an older/poetic enclitic form (vidi + mi) meaning 'I saw (before me)'; davante and sotto i piedi are fronted adverbial/location phrases ('before' / 'under my feet') that together locate the object; un lago is the direct object introduced after those adverbials. che begins a relative clause modifying lago; per gelo means 'because of/by reason of the frost'; avea is the imperfect ('had' / 'possessed') and governs di vetro e non d’acqua sembiante, literally 'an appearance of glass and not of water' (sembiante = 'appearance').
segments.inferno-32-g20: Non fece al corso suo: non negates the verb fece (past historic of fare, 'made/did'); al corso suo = 'over its course' (a + il). sì grosso velo di verno = 'so thick a winter veil' — grosso modifies velo, di verno ('of winter') specifies the kind of veil; the word order (adjective before noun in grosso velo) is idiomatic and emphatic.
segments.inferno-32-g21: né introduces a second negative clause ('nor'). Tanaï (the Don) là sotto ’l freddo cielo = 'there beneath the cold sky' — là sotto is a locative adverbial; ’l is the elided article before cielo.
segments.inferno-32-g22: com’ era quivi is a short clause meaning 'as it was there' or 'such as it was here/there'; com’ is the contracted com + e (as/how), era is the imperfect of essere ('was'), and quivi is a poetic locative 'there'. It links back to the previous comparisons.
segments.inferno-32-g23: che se ... fosse sù caduto = a hypothetical concessive clause: che se = 'for if' / 'that if'; Tambernicchi vi fosse sù caduto uses the imperfect subjunctive fosse plus caduto (past participle) to express a counterfactual 'had ... fallen down there from above' — vi = 'there', sù = 'from above'.
segments.inferno-32-g24: o Pietrapana is simply an alternative name appended with the vocative/alternative 'or' (o) naming another mountain; the phrase is a brief apposition continuing the hypothetical.
segments.inferno-32-g25: non avria pur da l’orlo fatto cricchi: non avria = 'would not have' (conditional of avere); pur adds 'even/still' for emphasis; da l’orlo = 'from the rim/edge'; fatto cricchi = 'made cracks/noise' with fatto as past participle of fare and cricchi as plural noun 'cracks' or 'crackings' — the whole = 'would not even have made a sound/crack from the rim.'
segments.inferno-32-g26: E come a gracidar si sta la rana col muso fuor de l’acqua: come introduces a simile ('as/like'); a gracidar si sta is an impersonal/reflexive construction meaning 'is in the act of croaking' (si sta + infinitive = 'is [doing]'); la rana is the subject and col muso fuor de l’acqua is a circumstantial phrase 'with its snout out of the water.'
segments.inferno-32-g27: quando sogna di spigolar sovente la villana: quando = 'when'; sogna = 'dreams' (present indicative, used generically); di spigolar = infinitive with di introducing the object 'of gleaning/picking up'; sovente modifies spigolar ('often'); la villana is the subject 'the peasant woman' whose dream is described.
segments.inferno-32-g28: livide is a one-word exclamation/adjective describing the colour/appearance of the shades: 'livid/ashen' — it stands alone for emphasis between commas.
segments.inferno-32-g29: insin là dove appar vergogna eran l’ombre dolenti ne la ghiaccia: insin là dove = 'even as far as the place where'; appar = archaic form of apparire 'appear' (3rd sing.); vergogna = 'shame' (here referring to private parts/shameful region); eran (imperfect of essere) + l’ombre dolenti = 'were the sorrowful shades'; ne la ghiaccia = 'in the ice' (ne = 'in', ghiaccia = frozen surface/ice). The clause thus locates the shades and their posture in the frozen surface.
segments.inferno-32-g30: mettendo i denti in nota di cicogna: mettendo is a present participle ('putting' / 'setting'); i denti = 'their teeth'; in nota di cicogna is an idiomatic simile 'in the note / manner of a stork' — note here means a sound or manner; grammatically the participle phrase describes the action/attitude of the shades.
segments.inferno-32-g31: Ognuna in giù tenea volta la faccia: Ognuna 'each one' is the subject; in giù = 'downwards'; tenea is the imperfect of tenere ('kept/held'); volta la faccia = 'turned her face' (volta is past participle/attribute here meaning 'turned'). The imperfect gives a continuous/action-in-progress sense.
segments.inferno-32-g32: da bocca il freddo: a short verbless clause where da introduces origin/source 'from the mouth' and il freddo is the nominative/theme 'the cold' — i.e. 'the cold came from the mouth.' This compressed phrasing is typical poetic economy.
segments.inferno-32-g33: e da li occhi il cor tristo tra lor testimonianza si procaccia: e da li occhi = 'and from the eyes'; il cor tristo = 'the sorrowful heart' (subject); tra lor testimonianza = 'testimony among them' (tra lor = 'between them'); si procaccia is a reflexive/passive-like construction (procacciare) meaning 'seeks/obtains for itself' or 'is procured' — the clause says the heart, through the eyes and mouth, seeks/produces mutual testimony among them.
segments.inferno-32-g84: This is a conditional clause introduced by se (if). Tu is the emphatic subject pronoun. Non vieni is present indicative with negation (you do not come / you are not coming). Venire + a + infinitive (vieni a crecer) expresses 'come to' or 'come in order to' do something; crecer is an archaic infinitive (modern crescere) meaning to increase/raise. La vendetta di Montaperti is the direct object phrase ('the vengeance of Montaperti'). Word order places the infinitive phrase after the verb venir(e).
segments.inferno-32-g85: Perché introduces the question ('why'). Mi is the clitic object pronoun 'me', and moleste is the second-person singular present indicative of molestare (you trouble/harass); together it means 'why do you trouble me?'. The clause is a direct question linked to the preceding conditional.
segments.inferno-32-g86: A single punctuation mark: the period/full stop ending the speech or sentence.
segments.inferno-32-g87: E io functions as the speaker label 'And I' (linking to the previous action). The pronoun io is emphatic and introduces the reported speech that follows.
segments.inferno-32-g88: Maestro mio is a vocative address: maestro (master/teacher/guide) followed by the possessive mio (my) placed after the noun for emphasis; it directly addresses the guide (Virgil).
segments.inferno-32-g89: Or qui = 'now here' (or = now, qui = here/at this point). M' (mi) is the clitic object pronoun 'me' attached by elision, and aspetta is third-person singular present of aspettare ('is waiting for' / 'awaits'), so m'aspetta = 'is waiting for me.'
segments.inferno-32-g90: Sì ch'io is a result/purpose clause 'so that I' (sì che + subjunctive). Esca is the present subjunctive of escire (archaic; modern uscire) meaning 'may go out / may emerge / may be freed' — here 'may get out of' or 'may rid myself of.' D'un dubbio = 'of/from a doubt'; per costui means 'concerning/about this man' (per used here to indicate the subject matter). The subjunctive in esca marks the result or purpose relationship introduced by sì che.
segments.inferno-32-g91: Poi = 'then' / 'afterwards.' Mi is the object clitic 'me.' Farai is future second-person singular of fare ('you will make / you will cause'); with the following noun fretta the construction fare fretta means 'to hurry (someone),' so mi farai = 'you will make me' (hurry me).
segments.inferno-32-g92: Quantunque here means 'however much' or 'as much as' (introducing the extent); vorrai is the future of volere ('you will want' / 'you will wish'), used idiomatically to mean 'as much as you wish' or 'as much as you like.'
segments.inferno-32-g93: Fretta is a noun meaning 'haste' / 'hurry.' In the phrase mi farai fretta it names the effect caused by fare: 'you will make me hurry' or 'you may hurry me.'
segments.inferno-32-g94: "Lo duca stette," uses the article/pronoun lo + duca (here 'the guide' or 'the leader') as the subject; stette is the passato remoto (past simple) of stare meaning 'he stood' or more idiomatically 'he paused/stopped'. The comma marks the short pause before the next clause.
segments.inferno-32-g95: e io dissi a colui che bestemmiava duramente ancora: — e = 'and'; io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'; dissi is passato remoto of dire, 'I said'; a introduces the indirect object (to); colui is a demonstrative 'that one/he'; che is the relative linking to colui; bestemmmiava is the imperfect 'was blaspheming/uttering curses' (ongoing past action); duramente is an adverb 'harshly/severely'; ancora means 'still' (indicating the blaspheming continued).
segments.inferno-32-g96: "Qual se’ tu che così rampogni altrui?" — Qual = 'what/which/who' in questions; se’ is the archaic/poetic form of sei (you are), indicated by the apostrophe (contraction); tu is the explicit subject 'you' (emphatic); che = 'who/that' introducing the relative clause; così 'so/in this way'; rampogni is present (2nd person singular) of rampognare 'you reproach/rebuke'; altrui 'others' (indirect object meaning 'other people'). The whole is a direct question: 'Who are you who reproach others in this way?'.
segments.inferno-32-g1: S’ïo is an archaic contraction of se io (“if I”), here introducing a counterfactual conditional; ïo = io (“I”). Avessi is the imperfect subjunctive (classical use) of avere used in the protasis (“if I had”). Le rime aspre e chiocce is a noun phrase: le (definite article) + rime (feminine plural) + two coordinating adjectives (aspre, chiocce) that agree in number and gender.
segments.inferno-32-g2: come si converrebbe is a comparative/result clause: come + impersonal si + conditional converrebbe = “as would befit.” Al tristo buco is the prepositional phrase indicating what it would befit (to the wretched hole). Sovra ’l qual pontan tutte l’altre rocce is a relative clause: sovra + il quale (sovra ’l qual) “above which,” pontan (archaic 3rd pl.) governs the relative subject “the other rocks” (tutte l’altre rocce).
segments.inferno-32-g3: Io premerei is the apodosis: io + conditional premerei = “I would press/press out.” Di mio concetto is an ablative/partitive phrase here meaning “from my thought” or “of my concept.” Il suco più pienamente: il suco (direct object, literal “juice” used metaphorically as “essence”) modified by the comparative/adverbial phrase più pienamente (“more fully”).
segments.inferno-32-g4: Ma perch’ io non l’abbo is a short sentence: ma = but; perch’ (perché) = since/because; io non l’abbo = “I do not have it” (abbo is an archaic form of avere). The clitic l’ refers back to the previously-mentioned quality (the rhymes).
segments.inferno-32-g5: Non sanza tema a dicer mi conduco is an idiomatic structure: non sanza = “not without”; tema = “fear”; a dicer (archaic infinitive for dire) = “to say”; mi conduco (reflexive-conduct) literally “I lead myself,” here meaning “I am led/compelled to speak” or “I venture to say.” Word order places the adverbial not-without phrase before the infinitive and main verb.
segments.inferno-32-g6: Ché non è impresa da pigliare a gabbo is an explanatory clause: ché = because/for; non è impresa = “it is not an undertaking”; da pigliare a gabbo is an idiom meaning “to be taken for a joke / to be treated lightly” (da + infinitive expresses purpose/attitude). Discriver fondo a tutto l’universo is the infinitival complement that names the task: discriver (infinitive) = “to describe” + fondo = “depth/bottom” + a tutto l’universo = “of the whole universe.”
segments.inferno-32-g7: Né da lingua che chiami mamma o babbo completes the negative: né = nor; da lingua = “by a tongue/language”; che chiami = relative clause with subjunctive/indicative sense “that calls” (present); mamma o babbo = informal words for mother and father. The speaker means that ordinary language (one that still calls “mamma” or “babbo”) cannot adequately describe such depths.
segments.inferno-32-g8: Ma = but; quelle donne (“those ladies”) is the vocative/accusative plural likely referring to the Muses or inspirational women. Aiutino is present subjunctive (3rd pl.) used optatively/hesitantly: “may they help.” Il mio verso = “my verse” (verso here = a line/poem). Ch’aiutaro Anfïone a chiuder Tebe is a relative clause (ch’ = che) using a past indicative form aiutaro (archaic spelling of aiutarono) “who helped Amphion to close Thebes,” giving a model for poetic help.
segments.inferno-32-g9: Sì che introduces a result clause: sì che = “so that.” Dal fatto il dir non sia diverso uses the subjunctive sia to express the wished-for result: dal fatto (from the deed) il dir (the account/word) non sia diverso = “that the telling not be different from the deed,” i.e. the poem should match the reality.
segments.inferno-32-g10: Oh is a vocative interjection. Sovra tutte mal creata plebe is a strong accusative/vocative phrase: sovra tutte = above all; mal creata = badly/ill-created (adjective + past participle used adjectivally); plebe = the rabble/common people. Che stai = relative clause addressing them directly (stai = 2nd sing. present of stare = “you stand/are situated”). Nel loco onde parlare è duro: nel loco = “in the place”; onde = “from which/whence”; parlare è duro = infinitive as subject meaning “speaking is hard.”
segments.inferno-32-g11: Mei foste state qui pecore o zebe is an exclamatory wish/condemnation. Mei (archaic) = meglio/better (i.e. “better that…”). Foste state is a compound (foste = 2nd pl. past tense of essere; state = past participle), here giving a counterfactual past: “you would have been.” Qui = here. Pecore o zebe = “sheep or goats” (animal imagery to shame them).
segments.inferno-32-g12: Come noi fummo giù is a temporal clause: Come = as/when; noi fummo = past tense (passato remoto) “we were/went”; giù nel pozzo scuro = “down into the dark pit.” Sotto i piè del gigante assai più bassi modifies location/relative depth: sotto i piè = under the feet; del gigante = of the giant; assai più bassi = “much lower” (comparative).
segments.inferno-32-g13: E io mirava ancora a l’alto muro: e = and; io mirava = imperfect “I was gazing” (mirare = to look at/admire); ancora = still; a l’alto muro = at/up to the high wall (a + articolo contracted: a + il = al, here a l’ for meter/poetry).
segments.inferno-32-g14: Dicere udi’mi is an instance of object-infinitive + verb of perception: dicere (infinitive) = “to say”; udi’mi (udi + clitic mi) = “I heard to me” (I heard someone say to me). The inversion emphasizes the heard words before reporting them.
segments.inferno-32-g15: «Guarda come passi: is direct speech. Guarda is the imperative (or present indicative with vocative force) of guardare = “look/see/observe.” Come passi = “how you step” (come + present subj./indic. passi describing the manner of movement).
segments.inferno-32-g16: Va sì is an imperative/instructional turn: va (imperative or present indicative used as command) = “go/move” or “walk on”; sì = “thus/so.” It introduces the manner the speaker wants the addressee to use.
segments.inferno-32-g17: Che tu non calchi con le piante le teste de’ fratei miseri lassi is a purpose/result clause introduced by che (so that). Tu non calchi uses the present subjunctive calchi after the imperative/resultive va sì (i.e. “that you not tread”). Con le piante = “with the soles” (piante = soles of the feet); le teste de’ fratei = “the heads of the brothers/fellows”; miseri lassi = two adjectives/epithets “wretched, weary.”
segments.inferno-32-g97: This segment is a punctuation mark (a period) alone as a quoted line break/pause in the poem; it carries no lexical material to analyze.
segments.inferno-32-g98: Or = 'now' (temporal/discourse particle). The clause uses an emphatic word order: tu chi se' = 'you, who are you' (chi se' is an archaic/informal order for chi sei: 'who are you'). Che introduces the relative clause che vai per l’Antenora = 'who go through Antenora'; per + definite article marks the place/region traversed.
segments.inferno-32-g99: Percotendo is the present participle/gerund of percotere ('striking'/'smiting'), used here adverbially to qualify the action of the subject in the relative clause (i.e. 'who go ... striking'). The -endo ending is the classical/medieval gerund/participle form.
segments.inferno-32-g100: Rispuose is the passato remoto (literary past) 3rd person singular of rispondere — 'he replied/answered.' It is inserted parenthetically to report the speaker's response between the two halves of the quoted speech.
segments.inferno-32-g101: Altrui le gote = 'others' cheeks' (altrui is an old possessive/indefinite meaning 'of others' or 'another's'); le gote is the direct object 'the cheeks' (plural). Word order places the possessive-like altrui before the noun for emphasis.
segments.inferno-32-g102: Sì che is a resultive connector meaning 'so that' / 'with the result that' and introduces the consequence clause that follows.
segments.inferno-32-g103: Se fossi vivo is a counterfactual conditional clause: se + imperfect subjunctive fossi ('if I were') + predicative vivo ('alive'). Fossi is the imperfect subjunctive of essere used for hypothetical situations contrary to fact.
segments.inferno-32-g104: Troppo fora? — troppo = 'too much' (adverb), fora is an archaic conditional/subjunctival form (from essere in older Italian) meaning roughly 'would be' or 'would be excessive'; together they form 'would be too much?' or 'would be going too far?'.
segments.inferno-32-g105: This segment is a punctuation mark (a period) marking a pause or end of a quoted line; it contains no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-32-g106: Vivo son io uses inverted/poetic word order: vivo = 'alive' predicate, son (sono) = 1st person singular present of essere 'am' (archaic form son), followed by the emphatic subject pronoun io. Together: 'I am alive.'
segments.inferno-32-g107: E caro esser ti puote is an impersonal/archaic phrasing: e = 'and'; caro = 'dear/pleasant' (predicate adjective); esser = infinitive 'to be'; ti = dative clitic 'to you'; puote (from potere) is 3rd person singular present meaning 'it can/it may' — literally 'and to be may be dear to you' i.e. 'and I may be dear to you.' The dative clitic attaches to the verb cluster as customary in older Italian.
segments.inferno-32-g108: Fu mia risposta uses fu (passato remoto of essere) = 'was', mia = 'my', risposta = 'answer' — 'was my reply.' This is the narrative insertion that frames the reported speech.
segments.inferno-32-g109: Se dimandi fama begins a conditional: se = 'if' (introducing the condition), dimandi is 2nd person singular present 'you ask/request' (archaic/dialectal form corresponding to chiedi), fama = 'fame/reputation' (object). Together: 'if you seek/ask for fame...'.
segments.inferno-32-g110: Ch’io metta il nome tuo tra l’altre note is a subordinate purpose/result clause introduced by ch’ (che = 'that'); io metta uses the present subjunctive (metta) after che for the speaker's volition/intent ('that I place/put'); il nome tuo = 'your name'; tra l’altre note = 'among the other notes/entries' (tra = among). The subjunctive metta expresses the intended action the speaker offers.
segments.inferno-32-g111: Ed is the conjunction 'and'. Elli is an archaic subject pronoun equivalent to modern egli/lui ('he'); a is the preposition 'to' and me is the object pronoun 'me' (here 'to me'), together introducing the reported speech that follows.
segments.inferno-32-g112: Del is the contraction di + il ('of the' / 'on the') used here in the fixed phrase del contrario = 'on the contrary'. Contrario is a noun meaning 'contrary/opposite'. Ho is the present indicative of avere ('I have') and io is an emphatic subject pronoun 'I' placed after the verb for emphasis. Brama (from bramare) here functions as a noun 'longing/desire' (i.e. 'I have a longing').
segments.inferno-32-g113: Lèvati is an imperative (or hortative) form meaning 'get away/leave' (literally 'raise yourself' used as 'go away'). Quinci means 'from here'. e is 'and'. Non introduces negation; mi is the clitic object pronoun 'me' (direct object with dar); dar is the infinitive 'to give' used after the negative imperative ('do not give me'); più here means 'any longer/no more'; lagna is a noun 'lament/complaint/whining' as the thing he is told not to give.
segments.inferno-32-g114: Ché is a causal conjunction 'for/because'. Mal is an adverb 'badly/poorly'. Sai is the second person singular present of sapere used with an infinitive to mean 'you know how (to...)'. Lusingar is an archaic infinitive form (lusingare) 'to flatter' written without the final -e. Per is 'by/with' (by means of), questa is the demonstrative 'this', and lama here denotes a slab/plate or blade (poetically used) — 'with/through this slab/blade'.
segments.inferno-32-g115: A single period marking the end of the preceding speech line; no lexical items to analyse.
segments.inferno-32-g116: Allor is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. Lo is the archaic clitic object pronoun 'him/it' (here 'him'). Presi is the passato remoto (past historic) of prendere: 'I seized/took'. Per is 'by' or 'by means of', la cuticagna = 'the nape' (the place by which he is grabbed). E is 'and' and dissi is the passato remoto of dire: 'I said', the colon introducing the reported words that follow.
segments.inferno-32-g117: El is an archaic clitic subject here functioning impersonally ('it'/'there'), converrà is the future of converire used impersonally 'it will be fitting/necessary'. Che introduces the subordinate clause; tu is the subject 'you'; ti is the reflexive clitic used with nomi; nomi here is present subjunctive (dependent on the impersonal converrà) meaning 'you name/call yourself' (i.e. 'that you give your name').
segments.inferno-32-g118: O che means 'or that' offering an alternative. Capel is an archaic form for capelli ('hair'), qui sù means 'up here' or 'on this spot above', non negates the clause, ti is a dative/clitic 'to you' (indicating whose head is meant), and rimagna is a present subjunctive form (from rimagnare) meaning 'remain' — together 'or that no hair remain on you up here.'
segments.inferno-32-g34: Quand’ is the elided form of quando (when). io is the emphatic subject pronoun. m’ is the clitic mi attached before the auxiliary ebbi; ebbi is passato remoto (I had) used here as the compound past auxiliary; visto is the past participle (ebbi visto = 'I had seen' / 'I had looked'). Dintorno and alquanto are adverbial modifiers: 'around' and 'somewhat'. Word order places the auxiliary before the participle as in standard compound past forms.
segments.inferno-32-g35: volsimi is the verb volgersi (volsi) with the enclitic reflexive pronoun mi attached: 'I turned (myself)'. a is the preposition 'to/toward'. piedi (plural of piede) is the noun 'feet' and here means 'at/to the feet' = 'toward their feet.' The clitic on the verb signals a reflexive/reciprocal turning.
segments.inferno-32-g36: e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. vidi is passato remoto 'I saw'. due is the numeral 'two'. sì is an adverb intensifier 'so/so very'. stretti is the past participle/adjective agreeing in gender/number with due (masculine plural) meaning 'close together' or 'tightly pressed.'
segments.inferno-32-g37: che introduces a result/consequence clause 'that/such that'. ’l is the contracted definite article il; pel = pelo 'hair' (poetic contracted form). del capo = 'of the head'. avieno is the imperfect/passé remote form of avere in 3rd person plural ('they had'); together with the participle misto it conveys 'their hair was mixed' (avere + past participle). insieme is an adverb 'together'; misto is the past participle 'mixed/mingled.'
segments.inferno-32-g38: Ditemi is the imperative (2nd person plural) of dire + enclitic mi: 'tell me' (addressing you plural). The form is direct speech imperative addressed to the addressees.
segments.inferno-32-g39: voi is the 2nd-person plural pronoun 'you'. che is a relative pronoun 'who/which'. sì is the adverb 'so' (intensifying). strignete is present indicative 2nd person plural of stringere 'you press/tighten'. i petti = 'the breasts/chests' (direct object); the phrase means 'you who press your chests so (tightly)'.
segments.inferno-32-g40: diss’ io is the contracted past-speech formula: diss(’e) = dissi/disse 'I said' (past tense) with the emphasized subject pronoun io. The apostrophe marks the elision used for metrical/poetic reasons.
segments.inferno-32-g41: chi is the interrogative pronoun 'who'. siete is present indicative 2nd person plural of essere 'are' (you are). This is a direct question in present tense inside the quotation.
segments.inferno-32-g42: This segment contains only punctuation (a single period) marking a pause or break in the verse; there are no words to analyze grammatically.
segments.inferno-32-g43: E is 'and'. quei is a demonstrative plural 'those' (referring to the people/figures). piegaro is passato remoto 3rd person plural of piegare 'they bent'. i colli = 'the necks'. Word order is subject (quei) + verb + object.
segments.inferno-32-g44: e poi = 'and then/afterwards'. ch’ is the clitic form of che introducing a temporal/subordinate clause ('after that/when'). ebber is passato remoto 3rd plural of avere used as auxiliary ('they had'); li visi 'the faces' (li = definite article), a me = 'toward me / to me'; eretti is the past participle 'raised/upright' (they had their faces raised to me). The whole phrase means 'and after they had turned their faces up to me.'
segments.inferno-32-g45: li occhi lor places the article li + noun occhi ('the eyes') followed by the archaic possessive lor (loro) after the noun: 'their eyes.' This is a topical fronting of the subject.
segments.inferno-32-g46: ch’eran = che + eran (imperfect 'they were'), introducing a relative clause 'which were'. pria pur here means 'just before / previously' (pria = before; pur = still/even), dentro = 'inside/within', molli is an adjective 'wet/moist' (agreeing with occhi): 'which had before been inwardly moist.'
segments.inferno-32-g47: gocciar is the infinitive 'to drip' used here in narrative/poetic diction as the verbal action attributed to the eyes ('their eyes ... to drip'); su is a particle 'upon/down onto'; per = 'over/onto' and le labbra = 'the lips'. Together: 'dripped down onto the lips.'
segments.inferno-32-g48: e = 'and'. ’l is the elided il (the) before gelo = 'frost/ice/cold'. strinse is passato remoto 3rd singular of stringere 'squeezed/tightened' (the frost tightened/contracted). le lagrime = 'the tears' (direct object). tra essi = 'between them' (i.e. between eye and lip or between the eyes); riserrolli is a past-form meaning 'they were rolled back / closed up again' (poetic verb, here conveying that the tears were frozen and sealed). The two verbs strinse ... e riserrolli describe the frost's action on the tears.
segments.inferno-32-g119: Ond' is an archaic contraction meaning 'thereupon' or 'whereupon' (literally 'onde'); elli is the older subject pronoun 'he' (modern Italian egli) and a me is the dative 'to me'. Together this is the reporting clause 'Thereupon he to me:' introducing direct speech.
segments.inferno-32-g120: Perché here introduces an interrogative/causal clause inside the direct speech and functions as 'because'/'for' (here: 'Because you...'). Tu is the subject 'you'; mi is the clitic object 'my'/'to me' (here 'my hair' or 'me'); dischiomi is an archaic 2nd-person present form of dischiomare — 'you tear out (hair)' — used literally.
segments.inferno-32-g121: Né begins a negative coordination 'nor / neither' continuing the refusal. Ti is the indirect object 'to you'; dirò is future indicative 1st singular of dire 'I will tell'; ch' is the clitic form of che 'that'; io is the emphatic subject 'I' (used before the verb); sia is the present subjunctive of essere 'to be' used after verbs of saying/knowing in indirect speech: 'that I am / who I am.'
segments.inferno-32-g122: Another negative clause introduced by né 'nor'. Mosterrolti is an archaic concatenation of the future mostrerò 'I will show' plus object pronouns (lo + ti) compressed onto the verb: 'I will not show him to you.' The form shows enclitic pronouns attached to the future verb in older spelling.
segments.inferno-32-g123: Se introduces a conditional concessive 'even if' / 'if'. Mille fiate = 'a thousand times' (fiate = occasions/times, an archaic plural of fiata). In sul capo = 'on the head'. Mi is the clitic 'me' (direct object). Tomi is an archaic 2nd-person form of a verb meaning 'to strike / to take' (here 'you strike me'); the whole clause means 'even if you strike me a thousand times on the head.'
segments.inferno-32-g124: Io aveva/avea is the imperfect (here spelled avea) 'I had' (archaic first-person auxiliary form); già = 'already'. 'I capelli in mano avvolti' places the direct object before the participle: 'the hair wrapped in my hand' — avvolti is a past participle used adjectivally ('wrapped').
segments.inferno-32-g125: E 'and' links clauses. Tratti here is a past participle meaning 'pulled' (context: had pulled/pulled out); glien' is the contracted gliene = 'from it / of it' (glie- + ne) referring back to the hair; avea is again the imperfect auxiliary 'had'; più d’una ciocca = 'more than one lock (of hair)'. Word order is typical older Italian with clitics and participles clustered.
segments.inferno-32-g126: Latrando is a present participle (gerund) meaning 'barking/howling' and modifies the subject (he); lui is the stressed pronoun 'he'. Con li occhi in giù raccolti is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with his eyes gathered/downcast' — raccolti (past participle) = 'drawn in' or 'drawn down', describing the eyes being cast downward.
segments.inferno-32-g127: Quando = 'when'; un altro = 'another (one)' (subject of gridò); gridò is the preterite/past historic 'shouted' / 'cried out' (3rd singular). The clause sets the temporal point at which the reported speech occurs.
segments.inferno-32-g128: Che hai tu is colloquial interrogative order: Che = 'what' (informal 'what is it that...'); hai is present 'have' (here used in the idiom 'che hai tu?' meaning 'what’s the matter with you?'); tu is the emphatic subject 'you'.
segments.inferno-32-g129: Bocca is a proper-name nickname formed from the common noun bocca 'mouth' and here addresses that sinner by his nickname; it functions as a vocative.
segments.inferno-32-g130: Non ... basta = 'is it not enough ...' (negated copular construction). Ti is the indirect object 'for you'; basta is 3rd-person singular of bastare 'to suffice'; sonar (infinitive) 'to make sound' or 'to rattle'; con le mascelle = 'with the jaws' (instrumental phrase). The phrase questions whether jaw-noise alone should be sufficient.
segments.inferno-32-g131: Se introduces the conditional/subordinate clause 'if / unless'. Tu is emphatic 'you'; non negates latri; latri is present (2nd-person) of latrare 'to bark/shout' meaning 'you bark'. The whole means 'if you do not bark' (i.e. 'unless you bark').
segments.inferno-32-g132: Qual diavol is an idiomatic exclamation 'what devil...' (qual = 'what/which'); ti is indirect object 'to you'; tocca is 3rd-person singular present of toccare used idiomatically to mean 'befall/afflict' (literally 'touch', here 'what devil has touched/afflicted you?').
segments.inferno-32-g68: E ('and') links this line to what just preceded; perché introduces a question 'why'. Non mi metti is 2nd-person singular present (negated) with the object clitic mi placed before the verb (typical in Italian): 'you do not put/mention me'. In più sermoni the preposition in + più ("more/any more") + the plural noun sermoni functions as an adverbial phrase meaning 'in more/other sermons'.
segments.inferno-32-g69: Sappi is the 2nd-person singular imperative of sapere meaning 'know' or 'be aware (that)...' and introduces the following subordinate clause. Ch’ is the contracted che ('that'). The i’ (here written i’) is the subject pronoun 'io' (I) in an archaic, apostrophized form; fu’ is the passato remoto (preterite) of essere 'was'. Il Camiscion de’ Pazzi is a nominal phrase: il + nickname Camiscion + genitive de' Pazzi ('of the Pazzi').
segments.inferno-32-g70: E ('and') continues the sentence; aspetto is 1st-person singular present of aspettare 'I wait for / I expect'. Carlin is the person awaited (proper name). Che introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that'; mi is the clitic object 'me' placed before the verb; scagioni is the present subjunctive (3rd sg) of scagionare 'to acquit/exonerate', expressing hope/expectation.
segments.inferno-32-g71: Poscia (afterward) opens the temporal clause. Vid' is the archaic contracted form of vidi (passato remoto 'I saw') placed before the subject io for poetic inversion; mille visi 'a thousand faces' is the direct object. Cagnazzi is an epithet 'doggish, mangy' modifying visi. Fatti is a past participle ('made/formed') that, with per freddo, explains cause: 'made by/because of the cold.'
segments.inferno-32-g72: Onde here functions as a result/introduction word ('whence' / 'therefore') linking the sight to the speaker's reaction. Mi is the dative/indirect object 'to me'; vien is present tense of venire 'comes'; riprezzo is a noun meaning 'a shudder/repulsion' — literally 'a revulsion comes to me.'
segments.inferno-32-g73: E ('and') links clauses; verrà is the future (3rd sg) of venire 'will come'; sempre is an adverb 'always/ever', emphasizing that the feeling recurs or will continue.
segments.inferno-32-g74: De’ is the contracted form of dei ('of the'); gelati is a past-participle adjective 'frozen' modifying the plural noun guazzi. Guazzi (literally 'puddles' / 'splashes') here denotes watery, filthy pools or wet masses; together 'of the frozen puddles' or 'from the icy splashes.'
segments.inferno-32-g75: E mentre ch’andavamo is 'and while we were going' (mentre + imperfect andavamo). Inver' lo mezzo is a contracted poetic form meaning 'toward the middle' (inver' = verso/inver towards) with lo mezzo 'the middle'. Al quale introduces a relative clause 'to which'; ogne (archaic ogni) 'every' + gravezza 'weight/burden' + si rauna (si + raunare) is a reflexive/impersonal construction meaning 'gathers itself' or 'converges.'
segments.inferno-32-g76: E io tremava is 'and I trembled' (tremava imperfect = ongoing past action). Ne l’etterno rezzo = 'in the eternal rezzo'; ne is used like 'in' and l’etterno rezzo is a poetic phrase meaning 'eternal chill/shade' (rezzo = coolness/shade). The whole expresses Dante's bodily fear during the approach.
segments.inferno-32-g77: Se here introduces an indirect question 'whether'; voler is used as a noun 'will/intention' (literally 'will'), fu is the past 'was', o ... o lists alternatives 'or destiny or fortune.' The speaker is admitting uncertainty about cause ('whether it was will, destiny, or fortune').
segments.inferno-32-g78: Non so is the simple statement 'I do not know' (non + present indicative so of sapere). It answers the preceding indirect question briefly.
segments.inferno-32-g79: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but', introducing a contrast or new point.
segments.inferno-32-g80: Passeggiando is a gerund 'strolling/going about', indicating an action simultaneous with the main verb. Tra le teste = 'among the heads' (tra = among; le teste = the heads), specifying where he walked.
segments.inferno-32-g81: Forte here functions adverbially ('forcibly/strongly'); percossi is passato remoto 1st person singular of percuotere/percosse meaning 'I struck' (I hit). ’L piè = 'the foot' (with the elided article), nel viso = 'into the face', ad una = 'of one' (i.e. the face of one of them). The sentence puts the agent (I) as speaker who struck a face with his foot.
segments.inferno-32-g82: Piangendo is a gerund meaning 'weeping' and expresses the manner in which the following action occurs. Mi is the object clitic 'me'; sgridò is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of sgridare 'he scolded/berated', so 'he scolded me, weeping.'
segments.inferno-32-g83: Perché introduces a direct question 'why'. Mi is the clitic object 'me' and precedes the verb; peste here is an archaic present form of pestare ('to trample/pound'), used interrogatively: 'Why do you trample on me?' The verb is in an older conjugation but functions as the present action in the question.
segments.inferno-32-g149: This is a conditional clause introduced by Se + imperfect subjunctive (fossi) + past participle (domandato) forming a passive sense: 'If I/one were asked'. The quoted question uses inversion and a contraction: Altri chi = 'who else' and v’era is a contraction of vi era ('there was').
segments.inferno-32-g150: A single comma as punctuation; it links this clause to the preceding conditional/question and marks the continuation of the speaker's reply.
segments.inferno-32-g151: Tu hai + past participle (dallato) is the present perfect (you have ...). Dallato is an archaic/poetic past participle here; the phrase quel di Beccheria = 'that one from Beccheria' uses di to indicate origin. Di cui is a relative construction ('of whom/whose') linking back to quel; segò (simple past) is the verb with Fiorenza as subject and la gorgiera as direct object.
segments.inferno-32-g152: Main clause: credo ('I believe') followed by a that-clause introduced by che + present subjunctive sia (used here because the speaker reports belief about someone's location). Gianni de' Soldanier is the subject; più là is an adverbial phrase meaning 'further on' or 'ahead', and con Ganellone e Tebaldello is a prepositional phrase 'with ...'.
segments.inferno-32-g153: Relative clause introduced by ch’ (che contracted) = 'who/that'; aprì is the simple past ('opened', here 'sacked' or 'took') with Faenza as object. Quando si dormia uses the impersonal/passive si with the imperfect dormia to mean 'when people/it were sleeping' (i.e. 'while it slept').
segments.inferno-32-g154: Eravam is an archaic/imperfect form (modern eravamo) of essere used as the auxiliary with the past participle partiti to indicate a prior action ('we had departed' / 'we had left'). Già strengthens it as 'already'. Da + ello (= from him/it) marks the source; ello is an archaic third-person pronoun referring back to someone mentioned earlier.
segments.inferno-32-g155: Ch' (che) introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that I saw…' (here temporal: 'when I saw'). Vidi is the simple past ('I saw'). Ghiacciati is an adjectival participle meaning 'frozen' (literally 'frozen ones'); in una buca = 'in a hole/pit'.
segments.inferno-32-g156: Sì che introduces a result clause ('so that'). L’un is contraction of 'l'uno' ('the one'), capo = 'head', a l’altro = literal prepositional phrase 'to/on the other' (here meaning 'upon the other'), era cappello = 'was a hat' (i.e. served as a covering like a hat). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect.
segments.inferno-32-g157: Come introduces a simile ('as/just as'). '’l pan' = il pane ('the bread'). Per fame = 'because of/out of hunger'. Si manduca uses the reflexive si to form an impersonal or passive sense: 'is eaten' / 'one eats it' — a general statement about how bread is consumed when hungry.
segments.inferno-32-g158: Così picks up the simile ('thus'). Sovran here means 'the upper one' (the higher of the two frozen figures), used as a noun. Li is the plural article 'the' (li denti = the teeth). Pose is the past historic (he/she/it put/placed). Là ’ve is a poetic contraction of 'là ove' = 'there where'. S'aggiugne is reflexive present meaning 'is joined' (the brain is joined to the nape) — a present factual clause giving the anatomical location.
segments.inferno-32-g159: Non altrimenti = 'no differently' / 'not otherwise', introducing the comparison. Tidëo (Tydeus) is the subject. Si rose (from rodere) is reflexive/middle voice used here to mean 'gnawed' (he gnashed/gnawed) and takes le tempie ('the temples') as the direct object; a Menalippo indicates the person at whom the action was directed. Per disdegno = 'out of disdain'.
segments.inferno-32-g160: Che here introduces an explanatory clause ('for/inasmuch as/whereas'). Quei functions as a demonstrative meaning 'that one' or 'the one in question' (subject of faceva). Faceva is the imperfect 'was making', so the action is described as ongoing (he was forming/making the skull). Il teschio e l'altre cose = 'the skull and the other things/parts' (elliptical for other parts of the head).
segments.inferno-32-g133: A single punctuation mark: a period ending the preceding sentence. There are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-32-g134: 'Omai' is an adverb/interjection (archaic/common in Dante) meaning 'now' or 'from now on' placed at the start of the quotation for emphasis; it is not inflected and functions to set the temporal/attitudinal frame of the speech.
segments.inferno-32-g135: 'diss' is the contracted, archaic past tense form (passato remoto) of dire (dissi); the verb precedes the subject 'io' here, a common inversion in reported speech: 'diss' io' = 'I said'.
segments.inferno-32-g136: Grammatically, 'non vo' che più favelle' contains 'non vo'' (short for voglio) + a subordinate clause introduced by 'che' with an implied second-person subject: 'I do not want that you speak any more.' 'favelle' is an archaic/poetic form of the verb 'favellare' (to speak) in a present/subjunctive-like form; 'più' here means 'any longer.'
segments.inferno-32-g137: A vocative phrase addressing the addressee; 'malvagio' is an adjective of reproach placed before the noun 'traditor' (archaic spelling of 'traditore'), forming 'wicked traitor.' Word order (adj. + noun) intensifies the insult.
segments.inferno-32-g138: 'Ch’' is the elided form of 'che' (here causal/introducing statement: 'for' / 'because' / 'that'); 'a la tua onta' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to your shame' (onta = dishonor); 'io porterò' is first-person future ('I will bring/carry') with the subject pronoun 'io' placed before the verb for emphasis; 'di te' means 'about you' or 'concerning you'; 'vere novelle' = 'true tidings/news' (novelle = reports). The whole clause states the purpose/result: the speaker will spread truthful reports that will dishonor the addressee.
segments.inferno-32-g139: 'Va via' is an imperative expression meaning 'go away' or 'begone.' 'Va' is the familiar/colloquial imperative form of andare; 'via' functions adverbially ('away'). The phrase is a direct command.
segments.inferno-32-g140: 'rispuose' is the passato remoto (third person singular) of rispondere, meaning 'he replied' or 'he answered'; the subject is omitted and understood from the context. The form is typical of narrative past in classical/poetic Italian.
segments.inferno-32-g141: This clause 'e ciò che tu vuoi conta' contains 'e' (and), the nominalized relative 'ciò che tu vuoi' ('whatever you want' / 'that which you wish'), and 'conta' (here functioning as an imperative 'count/consider' addressed to 'tu' or as a present-meaning 'it matters/it counts'). In the context of a command sequence, it's best read as a directive: 'and consider/reckon whatever you wish.'
segments.inferno-32-g142: 'ma non tacer' = 'but do not be silent.' 'ma' is contrastive ('but'), and 'non tacer' uses the infinitive 'tacer' (archaic infinitive of tacere) with negation to function as a prohibitive—an idiomatic negative command telling the addressee not to keep quiet.
segments.inferno-32-g143: 'se tu di qua entro eschi' is a conditional temporal clause: 'if you go out from here.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'se' (if) + explicit subject 'tu'; 'di qua' = 'from here'/'out of this place'; 'entro' here stresses movement 'out of the inner place' (in older usage); 'eschi' is an archaic/poetic second-person singular form of 'uscire' (to go out) meaning 'you go out' or 'you should go out.'
segments.inferno-32-g144: 'di quel ch’ebbe or così la lingua pronta' = 'about him who just now had his tongue so ready.' 'di' = 'about/regarding'; 'quel' = 'that one' (referring to a person); 'ch’' = 'che' introducing the relative clause; 'ebbe' = passato remoto of avere ('had'), here meaning 'was possessed of' or simply 'had' in the sense 'had such a ready tongue'; 'or' means 'just now' or 'a moment ago'; 'così' = 'so'; 'la lingua pronta' is an idiom 'a ready tongue' = quickness with speech, i.e., someone who was very ready with words.
segments.inferno-32-g145: El is an archaic subject pronoun equivalent to 'he' (egli); piange is present indicative 'he weeps/laments'; qui 'here' (locative); l' (il) argento 'the silver' (here meaning money or wealth); de' is the contracted form of dei 'of the'; Franceschi 'the French' (plural). Grammatically, a present-tense statement about someone lamenting the French money here.
segments.inferno-32-g146: "Io vidi", — Io 'I' (emphatic); vidi is passato remoto of vedere, 'I saw'. In quotation marks this reports what someone might say in the first person: 'I saw'.
segments.inferno-32-g147: potrai dir, — potrai is the future of potere 'you will be able / you may', used here to mean 'you might/could say' (a formula introducing a possible report); dir is the shortened infinitive of dire ('to say'), common in poetry where the final -e is dropped.
segments.inferno-32-g148: "quel da Duera là dove i peccatori stanno freschi" — quel 'that one'; da is the preposition 'from' (here indicating origin or association); Duera appears as a proper name (place or family) and functions as the object of da; là dove 'there where' introduces a locative relative clause; i peccatori 'the sinners' (definite plural); stanno is present indicative of stare meaning 'stand / are situated'; freschi is an adjective 'cool/fresh' describing the sinners' state (here likely 'in the cold place', so 'cool').
segments.inferno-32-g49: The phrase shows marked, poetic word order: 'Con legno legno' (literally 'with wood, wood') uses the instrumental 'con' + noun repeated for emphasis — 'wood against wood'. 'spranga' is a nominative noun (a wooden bar) placed before the verb. 'mai non cinse' is the passato remoto (cinse = cingere) with a two-word negation 'mai non' meaning 'never'; the adverbial 'forte così' ('so strongly') follows the verb to modify it. Overall: 'A wooden bar never bound wood so strongly.'
segments.inferno-32-g50: 'ond’ ei' (onde contracted to ond') is a connective meaning 'therefore/whence', and 'ei' is the archaic third‑person plural subject pronoun 'they'. 'come due becchi' is a simile 'like two beaks' (come = like). 'cozzaro insieme' is an archaic/poetic past action: cozzaro functions as the verb 'they butted/struck' (cozzare) with 'insieme' 'together' after it. Word order is flexible and compressed for the verse.
segments.inferno-32-g51: 'Tanta ira' is a noun phrase ('such great anger') where 'tanta' intensifies 'ira'. 'li' is the third‑person plural object pronoun 'them' placed before the verb (typical clitic placement), and 'vinse' is passato remoto of 'vincere' used intransitively here: 'overcame/seized'. The whole clause means 'such anger overcame them.'
segments.inferno-32-g52: 'E' is the simple conjunction 'and'. 'Un' = 'one' (someone). 'ch’' = che, the relative pronoun 'who'. 'avea' is the imperfect of avere ('had') used with the past participle 'perduti' (agrees in gender/number with 'orecchi') to mean 'had lost'. 'Ambo li orecchi' is an idiomatic phrase 'both the ears'. 'Per la freddura' uses 'per' to indicate cause: 'because of the cold.'
segments.inferno-32-g53: 'Pur' here means 'still'/'even so' followed by 'col' (con + il) 'with the' and 'viso' 'face'. 'In giùe' is an archaic form of 'giù' ('downwards'); the whole small phrase gives the circumstance 'still with his face downward.'
segments.inferno-32-g54: 'Disse' is the passato remoto (disse = dire) meaning 'he said', introducing direct speech; its placement at the end of the clause is the typical narrative tag.
segments.inferno-32-g55: 'Perché' here is an interrogative 'why'. 'Cotanto' = 'so much/so intensely'. 'In noi' = 'at/in us'. 'Ti' is the second‑person singular clitic 'you' (directed at Dante). 'Specchi' is used as a verb (present indicative, 2nd person singular) 'you look/you gaze/you reflect yourself' with reflexive 'ti' — poetically 'why do you gaze so much at us?' Note that the printed lemma/senses treat 'specchi' as a noun; here it functions as a verb (hence no matching noun sense).
segments.inferno-32-g56: 'Se vuoi saper' = 'if you want to know' — 'vuoi' (present of volere) takes an infinitive 'saper'. 'Chi son' = 'who (they) are' with the archaic plural 'son' = 'sono'. 'Cotesti due' is a demonstrative 'these two' (cotesti = 'those/these' in older Italian). The clause is conditional and introduces the identification that follows.
segments.inferno-32-g57: 'La valle' = 'the valley'. 'Onde' is a relative/locative 'from which/where' (here 'where'). 'Bisenzo' (Bisenzio) is the river name. 'Si dichina' is a reflexive present form of 'dichinare' meaning 'slopes down'/'bends'. 'Del padre loro Alberto' = 'of their father Alberto' (del = di + il). 'E di lor fue' repeats ownership/relationship: 'and was of them'—i.e. belonged to their father Alberto. 'Fue' is the passato remoto (was).
segments.inferno-32-g58: D’ is the contraction of 'di' = 'of/from'. 'Un corpo' = 'one body' (singular). 'Usciro' is an archaic/past form (passato remoto) of 'uscire' in the third person plural: 'they came out' or 'they issued forth'. The clause means 'They came out of one body.'
segments.inferno-32-g59: 'E' = 'and'. 'Tutta la Caina' = 'all of Caina' (Caina is the first subdivision of the ninth circle). 'Potrai' is the future of 'potere' (you will be able) followed by the infinitive 'cercare' (to search). Together: 'and you can search all Caina.'
segments.inferno-32-g60: 'E non troverai' = 'and you will not find' (future negated by pre-verbal 'non'). 'Ombra degna' = 'a shade worthy' (degna post‑nominal adjective). 'Più d'esser fitta' = 'any more to be fixed/stitched' (più = any longer/more, d' = di + esser). 'In gelatina' is a prepositional phrase describing where/how: 'embedded in gelatin' (here figurative).
segments.inferno-32-g61: 'Non quelli a cui' = 'not those to whom' (relative clause). 'Fu rotto il petto' is a passive periphrasis in passato remoto ('the chest was broken'). 'E l'ombra con esso' = 'and the shade with it' (ess o = the body). 'Un colpo per la man d’Artù' = 'a blow by the hand of Arthur' where 'per' here expresses agency/means ('by'). Word order places the agent phrase at the end.
segments.inferno-32-g62: A terse negative clause: 'non Focaccia;' — 'not Focaccia' (here 'Focaccia' is a proper name/nickname). The negation 'non' simply excludes that person from the preceding category.
segments.inferno-32-g63: 'Non questi che m’ingombra' = 'not these who encumber/beset me' — 'questi' = 'these', 'che' introduces a relative clause, 'm’' = mi (me) placed before the verb, and 'ingombra' is present indicative 'obstruct/encumber'. 'Col capo sì' = 'with the head so (much)', 'col' = con + il.
segments.inferno-32-g64: 'Ch’ i’ non veggio oltre più' is a subordinate clause ('that I no longer see beyond'): 'ch’' = che ('that'), 'i’' = io (subject 'I'), 'non veggio' = negative + verb (here 'veggio' is an archaic/poetic verb equivalent/functioning as 'see' or 'behold'), and 'oltre più' = 'any further/any more'. The sequence indicates inability to see beyond them.
segments.inferno-32-g65: 'E fu nomato' = 'and he was named/called' — 'fu' passato remoto of 'essere' + past participle 'nomato'. 'Sassol Mascheroni' is a nickname; the phrase states the name he was called. The clause is a nominative identification.
segments.inferno-32-g66: This short conditional/parenthetical address 'se tosco se’' = 'if (you) are Tuscan': the final 'se’' is an archaic/poetic form of 'sei' ('you are') placed after the adjective for dialectal emphasis. The phrase means 'if you are Tuscan'.
segments.inferno-32-g67: 'Ben sai omai chi fu.' = 'You well know by now who he was.' 'Ben' (well) modifies 'sai' (you know, present), 'omai' = 'by now', 'chi fu' = 'who he was' with 'fu' passato remoto. This is a direct assertion to the interlocutor that the identity should be known.
segments.inferno-32-g161: An address to the sinner: 'O' is the exclamatory particle. 'tu che mostri' is a relative clause ('you who show/display'), with 'mostri' in the present indicative. 'per sì bestial segno' uses 'per' = 'by/through' + 'sì' (so/such) modifying the adjective 'bestial' (beastly), and 'segno' is the noun 'sign/gesture' — literally 'you who show by such a beastly sign'. 'odio sovra colui che tu ti mangi' follows: 'odio' is the noun 'hatred', 'sovra' here indicates directed toward/against, and 'colui che tu ti mangi' is a relative clause 'the one whom you are eating'; 'tu ti mangi' is second-person singular present of 'mangiare' with the clitic 'ti' (poetic/reflexive/clitic usage here) giving the sense 'you devour him.'
segments.inferno-32-g162: 'dimmi' is the imperative 'tell me' (di- + mi) addressing the sinner directly; '’l perché' is the contracted form of 'il perché' meaning 'the reason' or simply 'why' — so together 'tell me why.'
segments.inferno-32-g163: 'diss' io' is the poetical past 'diss' (diss' = disse) + subject pronoun 'io' — 'I said.' The order places the verb before the subject (inverted) as is common in reported speech in poetry.
segments.inferno-32-g164: 'per tal convegno' is a short nominal phrase meaning 'on/for such an occasion' or 'in view of such an occurrence'; 'per' here gives the sense 'because/on account of' or 'concerning,' 'tal' is the shortened form of 'tale' (such), and 'convegno' literally 'meeting' but here 'occasion' or 'circumstance.'
segments.inferno-32-g165: This subordinate clause begins with 'che' introducing a reason/explanation ('for'/'that'). 'se' is the conditional 'if'. 'tu' is the subject 'you', 'a ragion di lui' is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'with reason because of him' or 'justly on his account' ('a' + 'ragion' = concerning/with regard to), and 'ti piangi' is a pronominal use of 'piangere' (to mourn/weep) with clitic 'ti' — here 'you lament/mourn him' or 'you mourn for him.'
segments.inferno-32-g166: 'sappiendo' is the present participle 'knowing' (used to introduce a concessive/circumstantial idea). 'chi voi siete' = 'who you are' (note 'voi' plural 'you' and 'siete' present 'are'). 'e la sua pecca' adds 'and his/its sin' — 'pecca' here means 'sin' or moral fault. The whole phrase: 'knowing who you are and his sin.'
segments.inferno-32-g167: 'nel mondo suso' = 'in the world above' ('nel' = in the, 'mondo' = world, 'suso' = up/above). 'ancora' here = 'still/yet'. 'io te ne cangi' literally 'I change you of it' — 'io' subject 'I', 'te' object 'you', 'ne' refers back to the matter just named (la sua pecca / that matter), and 'cangi' is first-person singular present of 'cangiare' (to change). In context the present is used with a conditional sense to mean 'I will (yet) change you about that' or 'I will alter your state concerning that in the world above.'
segments.inferno-32-g168: A closing conditional: 'se' = 'if' introducing the condition. 'quella con ch’io parlo' = 'that one with whom I speak' ('quella' feminine demonstrative, 'con' + 'ch’' = 'with whom/which', 'io parlo' = 'I speak'). 'non si secca' is a negative present reflexive construction: 'si secca' literally 'becomes dry/wither(s)' but idiomatically here 'does not fall silent/does not go mute'; 'si' is the reflexive clitic referring back to 'quella.'

sentences.inferno-32-s5: Whereupon I turned, and saw before me and under my feet a lake that by frost had the look of glass and not of water.

sentences.inferno-32-s1: If I had harsh, hoarse rhymes, as would befit that wretched hole above which all the other rocks hang, I would press out the essence of my thought more fully; but since I do not have them, not without fear I am driven to speak; for it is not an undertaking to be taken lightly to describe the depth of the whole universe, nor by a tongue that still calls “mamma” or “babbo.”

sentences.inferno-32-s4: As we went down into the dark pit under the giant’s feet, much lower, and I was still gazing up at the high wall, I heard someone say to me: "Watch how you step; go on like that, so that you do not tread with your soles on the heads of the wretched, weary brothers."

sentences.inferno-32-s45: “O you who by such a beastly sign display hatred against the one whom you are eating, tell me why,” I said, “on so strange an occasion; for if you, with good reason, mourn him, knowing who you are and his sin, I will yet change you on that matter in the world above, if the one with whom I speak does not fall silent.”
